coredata.dc:title: Co-production and upgrading of multiple products from hydrothermal carbonization of microalgae with organic solvent assistance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bin
Zhang, Jingmiao
Xia, Ao
Tang, Xinru
Zhu, Xianqing
Huang, Yun
Zhu, Xun
Liao, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal carbonization facilitates microalgae utilization but suffers from low product quality, with no effective solutions. This study investigated the product characteristics and quality enhancement methods of hydrochar, bio-oil, and carbon dots prepared through hydrothermal carbonization of microalgae assisted by non-polar organic solvents (petroleum ether and n-hexane) and polar organic solvents (ethyl acetate, isopropanol, and ethanol). The results showed that non-polar organic solvents were more effective than polar organic solvents in extracting fatty acids hydrolyzed from lipids into the organic phase, reducing amidation with amino acids and lowering bio-oil nitrogen content by 47.0%, with n-hexane exhibiting the best effect. They also promoted Maillard reactions, increasing hydrochar and carbon dots yields by 41.2% and 63.3% while boosting nitrogen content by 48.9% and 15.7%, respectively. This study explored the co-production and upgrading of multiple products in microalgae hydrothermal carbonization with organic solvent assistance, offering insights into process optimization for efficient utilization of microalgae.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Hydrothermal conversion
Organic co-solvent
Value-added processing
Nitrogen regulating mechanism

The synergistic effect of water and n-hexane on product yields
The effect of hydrothermal conditions on product yields
Morphology and optical properties of carbon dots
Reaction mechanism in the hydrothermal process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Separation methods for products prepared with different solvents.

Product yields from different reaction conditions. Product yields with different co-solvents, “Water” refers to the condition without co-solvent (2 h, 230 °C, liquid-to-solid ratio of 20:1, stirring speed of 200 rpm/min, and a co-solvent addition of 50 %) (a); Different n-hexane addition ratios (2 h, 230 °C, 20:1, 200 rpm/min) (b); Different hydrothermal conditions, denoted as reaction time − temperature − liquid to solid ratio − stirring speed (c).

Van Krevelen diagram and Sankey diagram of products. Van Krevelen diagram of hydrochar and bio-oil under different n-hexane addition ratios (a) and different hydrothermal conditions (b); Elements distribution from raw material to products using water as a solvent only (c) and adding 33% n-hexane as a co-solvent (d).
Morphology of Chlorella pyrenoidosa and hydrochar. SEM of Chlorella pyrenoidosa (a); SEM of hydrochar with different organic solvent addition ratios (b-f) and different hydrothermal conditions (g-o).

Morphology and optical properties of carbon dots. TEM and size distribution of carbon dots obtained with different n-hexane addition ratios (a-c); Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots from different conditions (d-f); Fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots from different conditions (g-i).
Reaction mechanism of multi-product co-production from microalgae hydrothermal carbonization.
Datasheet of four-factor and three-level orthogonal experiments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HC: hydrochar. BO: bio-oil. CDs: carbon dots.
Ki (i = 1,2,3): the total of the three experimental outcomes at each level. R: range.
Each experiment is conducted in duplicate.

head.ce:title: Co-production and upgrading of multiple products from hydrothermal carbonization of microalgae with organic solvent assistance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Low-grade Energy Utilization Technologies and Systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal carbonization facilitates microalgae utilization but suffers from low product quality, with no effective solutions. This study investigated the product characteristics and quality enhancement methods of hydrochar, bio-oil, and carbon dots prepared through hydrothermal carbonization of microalgae assisted by non-polar organic solvents (petroleum ether and n-hexane) and polar organic solvents (ethyl acetate, isopropanol, and ethanol). The results showed that non-polar organic solvents were more effective than polar organic solvents in extracting fatty acids hydrolyzed from lipids into the organic phase, reducing amidation with amino acids and lowering bio-oil nitrogen content by 47.0%, with n-hexane exhibiting the best effect. They also promoted Maillard reactions, increasing hydrochar and carbon dots yields by 41.2% and 63.3% while boosting nitrogen content by 48.9% and 15.7%, respectively. This study explored the co-production and upgrading of multiple products in microalgae hydrothermal carbonization with organic solvent assistance, offering insights into process optimization for efficient utilization of microalgae.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rising energy demand and worsening pollution have made research hotspots for renewable energy and green chemical processes. Biomass, due to its renewability and carbon–neutral characteristics, has gradually emerged as one of the critical alternatives to traditional fossil fuels (Yu et al., 2024). Among various biomass resources, microalgae are characterized by their small size, strong adaptability, rapid growth, and ability to thrive without competing with terrestrial plants for land (Hoang et al., 2023). The rapid growth of microalgae not only effectively treats wastewater (Chai et al., 2021) but also facilitates carbon dioxide fixation (He et al., 2024). Furthermore, microalgae can be converted into high-value products (Wu et al., 2023), driving growing interest in resource utilization.

Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a process that involves the decomposition and carbonization of biomass in a subcritical water environment at moderate temperatures (180–250 °C) and autogenous pressure. Compared to other techniques, this coal formation mimicking method does not require prior drying or other feedstock preprocessing, making it suitable for processing high-moisture biomass such as microalgae (Wang et al., 2024). The main product of microalgae HTC, hydrochar (HC), is rich in functional groups, exhibiting excellent porous structure and chemical reactivity (Wang et al., 2024). It has been widely utilized in adsorption, energy storage, and environmental remediation applications (Yu et al., 2024). The by-product bio-oil (BO), present as a water-insoluble organic phase, serves as a viable fuel source (Ağbulut et al., 2023). Additionally, the aqueous phase generated during this process contains small-molecule organic compounds as secondary by-products (Zhang et al., 2022). The lipid-rich nature of microalgae results in high BO yield during HTC (Hoang et al., 2023). Its high N content also endows HC with abundant N-rich functional groups (Huang et al., 2023), enhancing material applicability. However, it simultaneously elevates the N content of BO, leading to higher NOx emissions during combustion and severely limiting its suitability as a fuel. Moreover, the discharge of N-containing aqueous phases after hydrothermal microalgae treatment could lead to marine eutrophication, posing an environmental impact (Marangon et al., 2022). Besides, the traditional HTC process has limitations, such as low product quality and limited conversion rate (Ipiales et al., 2024).

Researchers have explored various approaches to utilize the aqueous phase generated from hydrothermal treatment, aiming to achieve high energy output while minimizing environmental impacts. Some researchers have combined HTC with other technologies, such as anaerobic digestion, to enhance the value of the products by utilizing the aqueous phase (González-Arias et al., 2023), while others have focused on recycling the aqueous phase post-HTC to improve the overall yield of products (Oliveira et al., 2022). Moreover, some researchers have used the aqueous phase as a plant fertilizer to promote plant growth (Spagnuolo et al., 2023). In addition, there has been considerable interest in the co-production of multiple products to add value to the HTC process. For example, microwave-assisted HTC of seaweed could co-produce HC, levulinic acid, and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (Deng et al., 2022). Similarly, hydrothermal pretreatment of wheat straw was investigated to produce oligosaccharides and fermentable sugars (Chen et al., 2018). However, studies on enhancing biomass utilization efficiency through co-producing HC (solid), BO (water-insoluble organic), and carbon dots (CDs) (aqueous), have not yet been reported. CDs are biocompatible, water-soluble zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with sp2/sp3 hybridized cores, widely used in environmental, medical, and chemical applications (Zhang et al., 2022). Existing studies have investigated the N migration and transformation mechanism during the HTC process of microalgae (Xiao et al., 2019). However, there is currently no research on controlling the distribution of elements in the co-production of multiple products, specifically to enrich more nitrogen into functional carbon materials (HC and CDs) while minimizing its presence in BO.

To improve product quality, some researchers have introduced organic solvents as co-solvents in the HTC process. Polar organic solvents could suppress the formation of nitrogen-containing compounds in BO (Liu et al., 2022) while simultaneously promoting the removal of oxygen by enhancing hydrogenation activity (Masoumi et al., 2021). The extraction effect of organic solvents may also help to optimize the distribution of nitrogen in the products and improve the product yields (Djandja et al., 2023). Non-polar organic solvents may extract intermediate products by constructing a biphasic reaction system, which helps inhibit their degradation and secondary reactions, thus surprisingly affecting product quality control (Cheng et al., 2023). Notably, the organic solvents added in the HTC process can be recovered during product processing and reused, maximizing economic efficiency (Weiner et al., 2016). Therefore, introducing organic solvents into the HTC process, particularly for microalgae conversion, offers a promising strategy to enhance product quality by regulating nitrogen distribution, optimizing reaction pathways, and mitigating undesirable reactions.

The primary objective of this study is to investigate the role of organic solvents in the HTC of microalgae, with a particular focus on analyzing their impact on the co-production of multiple products, including HC, BO, and CDs. This research explored the application of organic solvents as co-solvents in the HTC process, uncovering their potential to enhance product quality, regulate N distribution, and reveal the influence mechanism of organic solvents on reaction pathways during the HTC process. These findings are expected to provide new theoretical insights for developing biomass conversion technologies while reducing their environmental impact.

The microalgae used in this study was Chlorella pyrenoidosa (CP) powder, sourced from Fuqing King Dnarmsa Spirulina Co., Ltd, which was dried at 65 °C and then sealed for storage. All the organic solvents used in this study were of analytical grade. Ethyl acetate, petroleum ether, isopropanol, and dichloromethane were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., n-hexane was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and ethanol was sourced from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The HTC experiments were conducted using a 200 mL Hastelloy-made autoclave (JWCGF-200 mL, Xi'an Taikang, China). Magnetic stirring was applied throughout the entire experimental process. For each experiment, an appropriate amount of CP and solvent (deionized water, organic solvent, or deionized water/organic solvent) was mixed and loaded into the reactor, which was sealed with octagonal bolts. N2 was purged for 5 min to fully expel the internal air. The reactor was then heated at a rate of 5 °C/min to the preset temperature (200, 230, 260 °C) and maintained at that temperature for the designated reaction time (2, 4, 6 h). After the reaction, tap water cooled the reactor to room temperature.

Five different organic solvents were selected as co-solvents. Highly polar organic solvents such as ethanol and isopropanol, as well as the moderately polar organic solvent ethyl acetate, are miscible with water, whereas non-polar organic solvents like petroleum ether and n-hexane are not. Consequently, the separation methods for products prepared with different solvents vary accordingly (Fig. 1 ). When water was the sole solvent, the reaction mixture was filtered to separate the filter cake and filtrate. The filtrate was purified by 500 Da dialysis for 48 h and then freeze-dried to obtain CDs. The filter cake was extracted with dichloromethane, and the extracted liquid was subjected to rotary evaporation to obtain BO. The extracted filter cake was then dried overnight to yield HC. When the solvent was an organic solvent alone (n-hexane), the filtrate was extracted with deionized water and separated using a separatory funnel. The upper organic phase was subjected to rotary evaporation to obtain BO, while the remaining aqueous phase was purified by dialysis and then freeze-dried to obtain CDs. The filter cake, after being dried overnight, was defined as HC. When the solvent was a mixture of water and a non-polar organic solvent (petroleum ether or n-hexane), the filtrate separated into an organic and aqueous phase, which was treated as described above. When the solvent was a mixture of water and other organic solvents (ethanol, isopropanol, ethyl acetate), the filtrate was first subjected to rotary evaporation to remove the organic solvent. The remaining liquid was then extracted with an immiscible organic solvent (dichloromethane). After extraction and separation, the upper phase was used to prepare CDs, and the remaining liquid was used to prepare BO, following the previously described methods.

The effects of different organic solvents on microalgae HTC were compared to identify the most effective solvent under the reaction conditions of a reaction time of 2 h, a reaction temperature of 230 °C, a liquid-to-solid ratio of 20:1, and a stirring speed of 200 rpm/min (Zhang et al., 2022). Further experiments were then carried out with the same reaction conditions using varying amounts (0 %, 33 %, 50 %, 67 %, 100 %) of the selected solvent, aiming to systematically evaluate the impact of its addition on the co-production of multiple products and investigate the synergistic effects between co-solvents and water on products formation. Furthermore, an orthogonal experimental design was employed to optimize the yield and quality of multiple products in the HTC of microalgae using organic solvents as co-solvents and investigate the influence of hydrothermal conditions on co-solvent-assisted products generation. Four key factors were selected for investigation: reaction time, reaction temperature, liquid-to-solid ratio, and stirring speed. Each factor was set at three levels, with the level settings based on conventional hydrothermal conditions (Liu et al., 2019). The orthogonal experimental design and results are presented in Table 1 . The Ki (i = 1,2,3) value is the total of the three experimental outcomes at each level. The R-value represents the range, with a larger R indicating a more substantial impact of the corresponding factors.

The equations for calculating the mass yields of the hydrothermal products are as follows:
(1)
Yield of HC =
w 1
/
w s
× 100%
(2)
Yield of BO =
w 2
/
w s
× 100%
(3)
Yield of CDs =
w 3
/
w s
× 100%

Where w1 represents the mass of HC after drying, w2 represents the mass of BO after rotary evaporation, w3 represents the mass of CDs after freeze-drying, and ws represents the mass of the microalgae feedstock.

An Unicube elemental analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany) was used to determine the contents of C, H, N, and S elements in the feedstock, HC, BO, and CDs. The O content was obtained by difference. The formula for calculating the higher heating values (HHV) of the microalgae feedstock, HC, as well as BO, is as follows (Sun et al., 2018):
(4)
HHV
MJ / kg
= 0.33858 × C + 1.254 × H - 0.10868 ×
( O - S )
Where C, H, O, and S represent the mass percentages of C, H, O, and S in the sample, respectively.

The surface structure of the HC was observed using a Quattro S scanning electron microscope (SEM, Thermo Fisher, America), with the sample gold-sputtered for 30 s before observation. The morphology and structure of the CDs were observed using a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai, America), and the particle size distribution of the CDs was analyzed using ImageJ software. An FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) was used to measure fluorescence excitation-emission spectra and quantum yield (QY) of the CDs. The concentration of CDs in the solution was 0.02 mg/mL.

Qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of the organic components in BO were carried out using a Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometer (GC–MS, Agilent, USA, DB WAX column: 30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm). The injector temperature was set at 240 °C, the carrier gas (He) flow rate was 3 mL/min, and the split ratio was 100:1. The compounds were identified using the NIST20.L mass spectra libraries. Components with an area percentage less than 1.0 % were not presented but included in the total area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different co-solvents on product yields
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, compared to the control group (using deionized water as the solvent only), the assistance of polar organic solvents (isopropanol, ethyl acetate, ethanol) significantly increased the yield of BO, while non-polar organic solvents (petroleum ether, n-hexane) exhibited higher HC yields. This result is likely due to polar organic solvents donating hydrogen to inhibit free radical polymerization, reducing intermediate condensation and re-polymerization, thereby stabilizing intermediates, promoting dissolution, and increasing BO yield (Wądrzyk et al., 2023). By improving the generation and stabilization of the oil phase, polar organic solvents reduce the dissolution of products in the aqueous phase, leading to more organics depositing in the oil phase. Which directly resulted in an increased yield of BO and a reduction in solid products. The synergistic effect between the polar organic solvent and water could also contribute to this outcome (Lai et al., 2018). Non-polar organic solvents such as petroleum ether and n-hexane can dissolve lipids and hydrolyzed fatty acids but have low solubility for proteins, carbohydrates, and their hydrolysis products (amino acids, reducing sugars). As a result, phase separation allows for more Maillard reactions between amino acids and reducing sugars in the aqueous phase, leading to an increased yield of HC.

Among the selected organic solvents, n-hexane showed high yields of both HC and CDs. While n-hexane slightly reduced BO yield, it lowered N content, enhanced HHV, and improved fuel quality (see supplementary material). Therefore, n-hexane was selected as the most effective co-solvent for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic effect of water and n-hexane on product yields
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2b, adding n-hexane significantly enhanced the yields of HC and CDs. This effect is primarily due to adjusting the polarity of the reaction medium by n-hexane. Adding n-hexane lowers the overall polarity of the reaction medium, which helps non-polar or weakly polar intermediates (such as non-polar aromatic compounds) stabilize and aggregate or precipitate more effectively, forming solid HC. Additionally, the low boiling point of n-hexane allows the reaction system to reach subcritical conditions at lower temperatures (Masoumi et al., 2021). Under these conditions, the solubility and reaction rate of the reactants are enhanced, facilitating the formation and conversion of reaction intermediates, which in turn boosts the yields of HC and CDs. Furthermore, n-hexane can extract organic intermediates formed during the reaction. This extraction has stabilized certain intermediates like amino acids, preventing them from generating BO and promoting their further reaction to generate CDs or polymerize to form HC.

When the n-hexane addition ratio was 100 %, it demonstrated a markedly enhanced yield of HC. This is attributed to the significant reduction in the amount of liquid water within the reaction system, coupled with the complete vaporization of n-hexane at the reaction temperature (230 °C). This reduction weakened the hydrolysis reactions typically mediated by water in traditional HTC processes. Under these conditions, the organic components in the microalgae were more likely to undergo slow supercritical pyrolysis rather than hydrolysis. The pyrolysis process produced more solid carbonaceous products (Canabarro et al., 2013), and the resulting small molecules were easily further polymerized into CDs in the low-polarity gas phase environment.

However, operating under 100 % n-hexane conditions might lead to excessive reaction pressure and operational difficulties. Additionally, using a large amount of n-hexane would increase the solvent cost and complicate subsequent processing. An appropriate addition ratio helps maintain the stability of the reaction system while preserving some characteristics of aqueous phase reactions, making the process easier to control and optimize. Therefore, a 33 % addition of n-hexane was selected to optimize hydrothermal parameters through orthogonal experiments to balance the economic benefits and feasibility of the reaction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of hydrothermal conditions on product yields
ce:section.ce:para: According to Table 1, the R values for temperature and liquid-to-solid ratio were relatively large, indicating their significant impact on HC yield. The temperature had the most substantial effect, as it influenced the formation of HC through reactions like decarboxylation and dehydration. Higher temperatures provided more activation energy for these reactions, leading to the breaking of chemical bonds in the components, which promoted the further decomposition of solid residues (Liu et al., 2019). Ultimately, this resulted in a reduction in HC yield at elevated temperatures. The impact of the liquid-to-solid ratio was close to that of temperature, suggesting that the concentration of reactants played a vital role in HC yield. Stirring speed also showed a notable influence, indicating its role in ensuring proper dispersion and uniform reaction. It is noteworthy that reaction time had the least effect on HC yield, likely because the reaction stabilized in a relatively short time, and further extending the time until 6 h did not significantly increase the yield (Shen et al., 2024). Elevated temperatures promoted the degradation of lipids and other organic compounds in microalgae, resulting in the formation of BO (Kozyatnyk et al., 2023). Prolonged reaction times allowed more organic matter decomposition into oil-based products. From the R values, it was evident that both temperature and liquid-to-solid ratio significantly influenced the formation of CDs. While temperature was crucial for CDs formation, excessively high temperatures inhibited their yield. When the temperature was too high, it led to further carbonization of the carbon source or favored the formation of other products (Zhang et al., 2022). Thus, selecting moderate temperature conditions was beneficial for maintaining a high yield of CDs while preventing excessive carbonization.

The elemental analysis results revealed that non-polar organic solvents (petroleum ether, n-hexane) used as co-solvents enhanced the carbon densification of HC and BO while promoting deoxygenation, improving the HHV of the products (see supplementary material). Specifically, the HHV of HC obtained with n-hexane as a co-solvent reached 27.5 MJ/Kg, surpassing that of HC derived from poultry manure (17.1 MJ/Kg) (Ghanim et al., 2017) and co-hydrothermal HC from sludge and sawdust (13.5 MJ/Kg) (Zhang et al., 2023), indicating strong potential as a solid fuel. Additionally, non-polar organic co-solvents effectively reduced the nitrogen content in BO, which can mitigate NOx emissions during combustion, thereby enhancing the quality of BO. The change in product element distribution caused by the polarity of organic solvents may be due to the phase separation effect of non-polar organic solvents, which allows N-containing substances to be more concentrated in the aqueous or solid phases, leading to subsequent reactions that generate HC or CDs, thereby reducing nitrogen precursors in the BO formation process (Zhang et al., 2025). Based on these elemental analysis results and the previous yield data (Fig. 2), n-hexane was selected as the optimal co-solvent for further investigation of addition ratios and optimization of hydrothermal conditions.

The Van Krevelen diagram reflected the dehydration, decarboxylation, and demethanation processes. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the interaction between n-hexane and water significantly enhanced the dehydration and decarboxylation reactions of HC. For BO, although adding n-hexane increased the H/C ratio, it also reduced the oxygen content, resulting in a higher HHV. Along with the reduction in nitrogen content, the co-solvent effectively enhanced the quality of BO as a liquid fuel and reduced its impact on the environment. Temperature had the most significant impact on the quality of both HC and BO. As the hydrothermal temperature increased, the O/C and H/C ratios of HC decreased, indicating a higher degree of carbonization (Fig. 3b). This may be attributed to dehydration reactions removing hydroxyl groups, decarboxylation removing carboxyl and carbonyl groups, and the hydrolysis breaking down many ester and ether bonds (Zhang et al., 2019). High temperatures also promoted the decomposition or removal of oxygen-containing functional groups in BO (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups), facilitating the formation of carbon–carbon bonds in organic molecules (Li et al., 2022). This led to a reduction in oxygen content and further aggregation and densification of carbon atoms in BO, which is beneficial for improving its fuel quality. However, higher reaction temperatures imply more significant energy input, whereas the goal is to achieve a low-cost, high-yield process.

A Sankey diagram was created (Fig. 3c-d) to better study the distribution of elements after HTC. It can be observed that with the addition of n-hexane, the flow of elements like N and S from the raw material to the BO significantly decreased, while the C, H, N, and S content in HC and CDs noticeably increased. From an extraction perspective, n-hexane, as a non-polar organic solvent, tends to extract non-polar organic components like alkanes and long-chain fatty acids, which are low in N and S content. In contrast, polar organic compounds containing N and S (e.g., protein degradation products) are less soluble in n-hexane, leaving more of them in the solid product (HC) or dispersing in the aqueous phase, eventually participating in the formation of CDs. From a reaction standpoint, N-containing compounds (such as amino acids and other protein degradation products), which are more polar, have lower solubility in the organic phase, making them more likely to remain in the aqueous or solid phases. Under hydrothermal conditions, these nitrogenous compounds may undergo Maillard or Mannich reactions (Zhang et al., 2023), leading to the formation of N-containing polymers, which eventually solidify in HC and CDs (Xiao et al., 2019). Sulfur, commonly found in proteins and other sulfur-containing organics of biomass, decomposes under hydrothermal conditions, with some sulfur binding to organic carbon to form sulfurized organic compounds that, due to their higher polarity, do not easily enter the organic phase and instead contribute more to the formation of HC and CDs.

According to the SEM images of HC obtained with the assistance of different organic solvents, especially non-polar organic solvents, have a significant impact on the surface morphology of HC (see supplementary material). Fig. 4 b-f shows the SEM images of HC with varying addition ratios of n-hexane as a co-solvent. It was evident that as the amount of n-hexane increased, pits gradually appeared on the surface of the HC, with a notable increase in their number as the n-hexane ratio rose. The formation of these pits can be attributed to two main factors: dissolution and changes in the reaction mechanism. On the one hand, n-hexane dissolved lipids, fatty acids, and other non-polar organic compounds in the microalgae, extracting these substances from the solid phase (e.g., the HC precursor) into the organic phase (Bian et al., 2018). This extraction process reduced the amount of organic matter in the solid phase, leaving voids or pits on the HC surface after carbonization. On the other hand, n-hexane vaporizes under high-temperature conditions and does not participate in hydrolysis reactions with the aqueous phase, which may lead to localized processes resembling pyrolysis rather than typical hydrothermal reactions. Traditional HTC relies on the involvement of water, converting organic matter into HC through hydrolysis, dehydration, and condensation reactions (Yu et al., 2024). With the introduction of n-hexane as a co-solvent, the reaction mechanism gradually shifts from HTC to slow supercritical pyrolysis, allowing the organic matter in microalgae to be directly cleaved into HC and gaseous products (Canabarro et al., 2013). Compared to HTC, the shrinkage of materials and gas release during pyrolysis are more pronounced, making it easier for pits to form on the surface of HC. Under pure n-hexane conditions, the reaction primarily favors pyrolysis, leading to a significant increase in the yield of HC due to the solidification of more organic matter.

The SEM images of HC observed under different hydrothermal conditions (Fig. 4g-o) showed that as the temperature increased, the structure of the HC became more compact. This phenomenon can be attributed to promoting carbonization reactions at higher temperatures, accelerating the dehydration and condensation of organic matter in microalgae. Additionally, the reduction in secondary char deposits on the surface of HC can be explained by the enhanced hydrolysis of microalgae at high temperatures, leading to the generation of amino acids and fatty acids. This, in turn, facilitated the amidation reaction, where the amides generated combined with unsaturated fatty acids, promoting the conversion pathway toward BO (Ağbulut et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the TEM images and particle size distribution histograms of CDs (as shown in Fig. 5 a-c), it was observed that the size of the CDs prepared via HTC of microalgae mainly ranged in 4–7 nm, which is similar to the CP based CDs reported in the literature (Guo et al., 2021), but slightly larger than those derived from cotton straw (Liu et al., 2020) and bee pollen waste (Shan et al., 2022). Moreover, some CDs experienced agglomeration, resulting in particle sizes exceeding 10 nm. The addition of n-hexane enhanced the degree of carbonization, leading to an increase in CDs size, as shown in Fig. 5b-c (Sun et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs produced in the co-production process showed excitation-wavelength dependence, with a redshift observed as the excitation wavelength increased (Fig. 5d-f), which is typically related to changes in the surface states and band gap of CDs (Zhang et al., 2022). As the excitation wavelength increased to 340 nm (in some cases 360 nm, see supplementary material), the photoluminescence intensity was positively correlated with the excitation wavelength. Beyond this point, further increases in the excitation wavelength resulted in a negative correlation. This behavior is consistent with the experimental observation that these CDs emitted blue or blue-violet fluorescence when excited by UV light. However, in two cases—where ethyl acetate was used as a co-solvent (2 h, 230 °C, 20:1, 200 rpm/min) and n-hexane was used as a co-solvent (2 h, 200 °C, 10:1, 100 rpm/min)—the strongest fluorescence was observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Ethyl acetate, a polar organic solvent, may introduce polar groups such as carboxyl or ester groups on the surface of the CDs, altering their surface states and thus influencing their emission behavior (Tang et al., 2019). Additionally, the increase in reaction temperature caused a blue shift in the maximum emission peak (Zhang et al., 2016). The fluorescence emission characteristics of the CDs may be attributed to variations in the size of the CDs and the heteroatom functional groups present on their surfaces (Zhu et al., 2022).

The photoluminescence QY of CDs produced under different conditions are shown in Fig. 5g-i. Non-polar organic solvents aided in dissolving the non-polar components of microalgae, facilitating the breakdown and extraction of organic materials, resulting in CDs with more uniform structure and higher QY. The QY of CDs with varying ratios of n-hexane as a co-solvent also significantly enhanced fluorescence properties. Notably, the optimization of reaction conditions through orthogonal experiments resulted in the highest QY of 12.7 %, which is considerably higher than other biomass-based CDs such as those derived from bagasse (4.5 %) (Boruah et al., 2020), bee pollen (4.8 %) (Shan et al., 2022), and mango peels (8.5 %) (Jiao et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism in the hydrothermal process
ce:section.ce:para: The potential mechanism of multi-product co-production from the HTC of microalgae is illustrated in Fig. 6 . The generation pathways of HC primarily include two routes: solid–solid conversion and soluble pathways (Evcil et al., 2020). The solid–solid conversion refers to the primary char formed directly from the carbonization of the skeletal structure (such as insoluble proteins) after the hydrolysis of microalgae. The soluble pathway involves the hydrolysis of carbohydrates into soluble monomers, which further decompose into furan units. These furan units then undergo condensation or polymerization reactions to form secondary char particles. The secondary char is captured by the primary char, ultimately resulting in HC (Shen et al., 2024). CDs are mainly produced through the hydrolysis of microalgae, followed by Amadori rearrangement, cyclization/aromatization, and polymerization reactions (Zhang et al., 2022). The formation of BO primarily stems from lipid hydrolysis, where the hydrolysis products, glycerol and fatty acids, undergo decarboxylation to generate alkanes and alkenes. These decarboxylation products, along with fatty acids and amides formed via the amidation reaction between fatty acids and amino acids, serve as the key constituents of BO (Ağbulut et al., 2023).

Adding n-hexane extracted the non-polar components of microalgae (such as fatty acids and lipids) into the organic phase, making these substances less likely to interact with amino acids in the aqueous phase (Zhang et al., 2022). The amide formation reaction of N-containing compounds from amino acids in the microalgae was weakened, which was consistent with the GC–MS results showing no detected amides in the BO produced with n-hexane assistance (see supplementary material). Consequently, more N-containing compounds, such as amino acids, remained in the solid phase or dissolved in water. Some undissolved N-containing compounds in the solid phase underwent solid–solid conversion to form primary char, while those in the aqueous phase participated in condensation reactions to generate secondary char or underwent cyclization, rearrangement, and polymerization to form CDs (Evcil et al., 2020). As a result, nitrogen was concentrated in the solid products while decreasing in the BO, which was consistent with the elemental analysis results (see supplementary material). Additionally, the vaporization effect of n-hexane reduced the influence of hydrolysis, causing the organic components of microalgae to undergo slow pyrolysis more readily at high temperatures (Yang et al., 2019). This effect promoted the cleavage of unsaturated fatty acids into short-chain alkanes, altering the composition of BO. In HTC without n-hexane, BO mainly consisted of long-chain fatty acids such as palmitic acid (n-hexadecanoic acid) and linoleic acid ((Z, Z)-9,12-Octadecadienoic acid). When n-hexane was introduced to assist HTC, the content of alkanes (e.g., 3-methylpentane) and ketones (e.g., acetone) increased. Carboxylic acids were also major cracking products, and due to their polarity, short-chain carboxylic acids accumulated in the aqueous phase after introducing n-hexane, with hexanoic acid emerging as the predominant compound (see supplementary material). The shift in mechanism also intensified the decomposition and carbonization of organic matter in microalgae, forming a denser carbon framework. During pyrolysis, the cleavage of CH bonds in the organic matter released significant amounts of gases (CO2, H2O, etc.), reducing the oxygen content in the HC, thereby increasing the carbon content. As a result, the degree of carbonization was enhanced, and the HHV of the HC improved. The role of n-hexane facilitated dehydration, condensation, and carbonization reactions within the system. On the one hand, it enhanced cyclization and aromatization reactions, contributing to the formation of more highly carbonized aromatic structures. On the other hand, it promoted the conversion of carboxyl groups (–COOH) at the edges of CDs into carbonyl groups (−CO) (Tang et al., 2019). Carboxyl groups typically act as non-radiative recombination centers, leading to increased non-radiative recombination. The reduction of carboxyl groups lowered the probability of non-radiative transitions, while the increase in carbonyl groups improved the likelihood of radiative transitions, which could enhance the fluorescence QY of the CDs (Fig. 5) (Mei and Zhang, 2012).

Different organic solvents were chosen to modify the production of hydrochar, bio-oil, and carbon dots from microalgae hydrothermal carbonization. Non-polar organic solvents improved fuel performances of hydrochar while increasing fluorescence properties of carbon dots. They could reduce bio-oil N content, minimizing its environmental impact as fuel, while enriching N in hydrochar and carbon dots for functional applications, with n-hexane performing best. By extracting fatty acids, non-polar organic solvents inhibited their amidations with amino acids, preserving amino acids for aqueous-phase reactions that boosted hydrochar and carbon dots yields. Overall, n-hexane enhanced microalgae hydrothermal carbonization efficiency, offering a promising biomass utilization strategy.

Bin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jingmiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ao Xia: Writing – review & editing. Xinru Tang: Investigation. Xianqing Zhu: Investigation. Yun Huang: Investigation. Xun Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qiang Liao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52236009, 52021004, 52106224), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTB2023NSCQ-JQX0005), the Permanent Staff Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Low-grade Energy Utilization Technologies and Systems permanent staff research fund (No.LLEUTS-2024001), and the Venture & Innovation Support Program for Chongqing Overseas Returnees (No. cx2022027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2025.132514.
